evidence: Contract identity primitives are defined in `goseg/protocol/actionids/actionids.go`, then re-exported in `goseg/protocol/contracts/protocolfamily/specs.go` (`ProtocolActionContractRoot`, namespace/action constants, and derived ID constants), creating more than one authoritative boundary surface.

`goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/fixtures_test.go` validates contract fixtures against `groundseg/protocol/actions` runtime constants, while contract-family declarations in `goseg/protocol/contracts/protocolfamily/specs.go` are built from `actionids` + `familyspec`; this mixes runtime action API and contract-governance API in the conformance boundary.

`README.md` defines backend quality gate as `go test ./...` and references external docs for contract-gate mapping, while contract-governance enforcement is implemented in dedicated tests (`goseg/protocol/contracts/family_specs_test.go`, `goseg/protocol/contracts/conformance/fixtures_test.go`) that are not explicitly surfaced as a named architecture contract gate.

findings.summary: Protocol and uploadsvc tests are mutually coupled, creating boundary-crossing change blast radius.
evidence: `family_specs_test.go` validates protocol catalog behavior using `uploadsvc/contractspec.UploadActionSpecs()`, so protocol tests depend on uploadsvc package details.
`contractspec_test.go` validates uploadsvc behavior by enumerating `contracts.ActionContractBindings()` from protocol contracts, so uploadsvc tests depend on protocol catalog internals.
This two-way assertion pattern means a local contract change in either package requires synchronized edits in both test suites.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single contract-governance test seam (for example, a shared conformance fixture package under `protocol/contracts/conformance`) that owns cross-package assertions. Keep feature-package tests focused on local parsing/behavior and remove direct protocol<->feature mutual assertions.

findings.summary: Contract IDs and action specs are distributed across multiple packages with no single authoritative module.
evidence: `actionids.go` defines canonical namespace/action/contract-id constants, while `protocolfamily/specs.go` and `uploadsvc/contractspec/specs.go` each rebuild family-specific catalogs by re-exporting and recombining those constants.

Conformance tests (`fixtures_test.go` and related tests) are required to keep these parallel declarations aligned, indicating coordination through duplicated declarations rather than a single owning boundary.
Adding or changing an action currently requires touching multiple modules and tests to preserve consistency, increasing architectural drift risk.
findings.suggestion: Create one authoritative contract catalog package that owns namespace/action/ID/spec metadata, and have protocolfamily/uploadsvc expose thin views generated from that catalog. Keep conformance tests as verification, not as the primary mechanism stitching duplicate declarations together.

findings.summary: Contract IDs and action vocabulary are maintained through duplicated boundary surfaces instead of a single authoritative module.
evidence: `actionids.go` defines root, namespaces, actions, and composed contract IDs.
`protocolfamily/specs.go` re-exports these constants under new names and uses them as family declarations.
`family_specs_test.go` also constructs validation expectations through separate contract constants/metadata paths, reinforcing multiple parallel representations for the same contract vocabulary.
findings.suggestion: Establish one canonical contract identity source (for root/namespace/action/contract ID), make family specs consume it directly without re-export aliases, and enforce a no-duplicate-constant rule in contract packages.

findings.summary: Conformance fixtures are coupled to runtime action package constants, blurring contract-governance boundaries.
evidence: `fixtures_test.go` imports `groundseg/protocol/actions` and checks fixture keys against `actions.NamespaceC2C` and `actions.ActionC2CConnect`.
Contract family declarations in `protocolfamily/specs.go` are expressed through contract-focused types (`familyspec.ActionSpec`) and `actionids`, indicating a separate governance boundary.
This creates dependency direction where contract conformance is validated against runtime action API rather than solely against canonical contract declarations.
findings.suggestion: Drive conformance validation from canonical contract declarations (`actionids`/family specs) and keep runtime `actions` compatibility checks in a dedicated adapter test layer to preserve boundary direction.

findings.summary: Repository-level quality gate policy does not explicitly name the contract conformance gate it relies on for compatibility governance.
evidence: `README.md` backend gate requires only `go test ./...` and links to external documentation for detailed contract-gate mapping.

Contract governance is enforced by specific test suites (`TestActionContractFixturesMatchCanonicalBindings`, `TestProtocolFamilyDeclarationsValidateAgainstGovernance`, `TestStartramFamilyDeclarationsValidateAgainstGovernance`) but this gate is not explicitly named in root policy text.
Without explicit top-level policy naming, architecture compatibility contracts are discoverable only by reading test internals or secondary docs.
findings.suggestion: Add an explicit contract-conformance gate section in `README.md` (with required command/target and ownership), and align it with the existing contract test suite naming to make boundary policy auditable at repo root.
